content.text: Rollout Pause and Rollback Trigger Criteria

A gradual rollout without defined stop criteria is not a safety mechanism — it is a delayed full deployment. The value of ringed and canary strategies comes entirely from having explicit thresholds that stop or reverse the rollout when something goes wrong. Without those thresholds, a team will debate whether a metric looks bad enough to act, losing the window where rolling back is low-cost.

This document defines the three-tier severity model (Advisory, Major, Critical), the specific metric thresholds that trigger each tier, and the precise actions taken at each tier — including how to automate pause and rollback so that human decision latency does not determine blast radius.

attrs.alt: Decision tree: Metric Breach Detected → Assess Severity → ADVISORY (continue + alert), MAJOR (pause rollout, investigate, fix, resume or abort), CRITICAL (immediate rollback, PagerDuty, post-mortem)

content.text: Every metric breach falls into one of three tiers. The tier determines the action taken immediately — no deliberation required. The rationale for having three tiers rather than two (rollback or continue) is that many breaches are transient or caused by factors unrelated to the new version, and a full rollback has its own cost: user disruption, lost instrumentation data, and a delayed release.
Halt ring advancement, freeze at current percentage

Critical tier rollbacks should be automated. If a human must approve a rollback, the time between detection and action grows to 5–15 minutes, during which all users on the canary are affected. Define the critical threshold conservatively enough that false positives are rare, then automate the response.

Traditional services use error rate, latency, and availability as the primary rollout signals. GenAI services add eval quality, safety classification, and token cost — three dimensions that have no analogue in conventional web services and require dedicated instrumentation during the rollout.

The eval suite runs continuously against the canary service during rollout, typically as a background job that replays a subset of golden test cases against the live environment every 5 minutes. The eval pass rate is the fraction of those cases where the canary response matches the expected behavior.
Pass rate drops 2pp below baseline for >10 min
Auto-rollback even if first window was borderline

Distinguish between eval categories when assessing severity. A 5pp drop in format compliance (the model returns valid JSON but with extra whitespace) is different from a 5pp drop in factual correctness or safety classification. The MAJOR threshold should evaluate across all categories, but the CRITICAL threshold should trigger if safety or factual accuracy drops — not just overall pass rate.

A safety classifier or PII detector runs on every canary response. The flag rate is the fraction of responses that trigger a safety or PII detection event. Because safety failures are high-severity by definition, the CRITICAL threshold for safety is much lower than for other metrics.
Elevated but may be user distribution skew
Safety flag rate > 0.2% for any window
Rollback — any sustained safety regression is unacceptable
Any single CSAM or illegal content flag
Immediate rollback + escalation to Trust & Safety team

Latency regressions in GenAI services have two common causes: a new model is larger or runs more tokens, or a new prompt template produces longer output. Both are predictable in shadow mode, so a large latency regression at canary time usually indicates something was missed in pre-production testing.
Monitor; check if token count increased
Pause; investigate model version or prompt length
Rollback — users are experiencing timeout-level waits
P99 > 5000 ms absolute for any window

HTTP error rate is the standard reliability signal. For GenAI services, distinguish between infrastructure errors (timeouts, OOM, upstream LLM API errors) and application-layer errors (malformed responses, downstream parse failures). Infrastructure errors trigger rollback faster because they affect all users equally.
Pause; check if LLM API or downstream parser is erroring
Error rate > 5% absolute for any 1-min window

Cost spikes in GenAI services can be caused by prompt injection attacks (users crafting inputs that expand token count), a new prompt template that is longer than the old one, or a model that produces more verbose completions. Cost is not a real-time signal — it is computed as a projection based on the current token rate extrapolated to 24 hours.
Pause; inspect token counts per request
Rollback — likely prompt injection or prompt regression

# prometheus/rollout_triggers.yaml
groups:
  - name: canary_rollout_triggers
    rules:
      # CRITICAL: Eval quality collapse
      - alert: CanaryEvalCritical
        expr: |
          genai_eval_pass_rate{slot="canary"} < 0.88
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          action: rollback
        annotations:
          summary: "Canary eval pass rate below 88% for 5m — trigger rollback"
          runbook: "python scripts/canary_rollback.py --reason eval_critical"

      # CRITICAL: Safety spike
      - alert: CanarySafetyCritical
        expr: |
          rate(genai_safety_flags_total{slot="canary"}[5m])
          / rate(genai_requests_total{slot="canary"}[5m]) > 0.002
        for: 0m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          action: rollback
        annotations:
          summary: "Canary safety flag rate exceeded 0.2% — immediate rollback"

      # MAJOR: Latency regression
      - alert: CanaryLatencyMajor
        expr: |
          histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(genai_request_duration_seconds_bucket{slot="canary"}[5m]))
          - histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(genai_request_duration_seconds_bucket{slot="stable"}[5m])) > 0.5
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: major
          action: pause
        annotations:
          summary: "Canary P99 latency >500ms above stable for 10m — pause rollout"

      # MAJOR: Eval degradation (pause threshold)
      - alert: CanaryEvalMajor
        expr: |
          genai_eval_pass_rate{slot="canary"} < 0.93
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: major
          action: pause
        annotations:
          summary: "Canary eval pass rate below 93% — pause ring advancement"

# alertmanager/config.yaml (relevant routing section)
route:
  receiver: default
  routes:
    - match:
        action: rollback
        severity: critical
      receiver: auto-rollback
      continue: false   # do not also page the human; automation acts first

    - match:
        action: pause
        severity: major
      receiver: pause-and-page

receivers:
  - name: auto-rollback
    webhook_configs:
      - url: "http://deployment-controller:8080/rollback"
        send_resolved: false

  - name: pause-and-page
    webhook_configs:
      - url: "http://deployment-controller:8080/pause"
    pagerduty_configs:
      - service_key: "{{ env "PAGERDUTY_CANARY_KEY" }}"
        description: "Canary major incident — ring advancement paused"

# app/deployment/rollback_controller.py
import logging, time, subprocess
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class RollbackReason(str, Enum):
    EVAL_CRITICAL = "eval_critical"
    SAFETY_CRITICAL = "safety_critical"
    LATENCY_CRITICAL = "latency_critical"
    ERROR_RATE_CRITICAL = "error_rate_critical"
    COST_CRITICAL = "cost_critical"
    MANUAL = "manual"

@dataclass
class RollbackEvent:
    reason: RollbackReason
    triggered_by: str  # "prometheus_alert" or "manual:engineer_name"
    metric_value: float
    threshold: float

def execute_rollback(event: RollbackEvent, feature_flag_key: str) -> None:
    log = logging.getLogger("rollback_controller")
    log.critical(
        "ROLLBACK triggered: reason=%s value=%.4f threshold=%.4f triggered_by=%s",
        event.reason, event.metric_value, event.threshold, event.triggered_by
    )

    # 1. Flip feature flag to 0% (atomic, all users immediately get stable)
    subprocess.check_call([
        "launchdarkly-cli", "flag", "set",
        "--flag", feature_flag_key,
        "--value", "false",          # disables canary routing entirely
        "--comment", f"auto-rollback: {event.reason}",
    ])

    # 2. Verify routing has switched (poll for 30s)
    deadline = time.time() + 30
    while time.time() < deadline:
        out = subprocess.check_output([
            "launchdarkly-cli", "flag", "get", "--flag", feature_flag_key
        ])
        if b'"value": false' in out:
            log.info("Feature flag confirmed disabled — canary traffic is zero")
            break
        time.sleep(2)
    else:
        log.error("Flag did not confirm within 30s — manual intervention required")

    # 3. Fire PagerDuty incident (separate from Alertmanager routing)
    _fire_pagerduty_incident(event)

def execute_pause(feature_flag_key: str, reason: str) -> None:
    log = logging.getLogger("rollback_controller")
    log.warning("PAUSE triggered: reason=%s", reason)
    # Pause = stop ring advancement; do NOT change current traffic percentage
    # Write a "paused" flag to deployment state store
    subprocess.check_call([
        "redis-cli", "set", f"canary:paused:{feature_flag_key}", "1", "EX", "86400"
    ])
    log.info("Ring advancement paused for 24h — on-call must resume or abort")
The Pause State: What It Means and How to Exit

A paused rollout holds traffic at the current ring percentage. Users already on the canary continue receiving new responses; new users are not added to the canary cohort. This is distinct from a rollback — the canary service keeps running.
Pause is appropriate when the breach is likely transient or investigable within hours. Rollback is appropriate when the root cause is unknown and the ongoing user impact cannot be bounded.
Before resuming a paused rollout, the team must confirm all of the following:
Root cause of the breach is identified and documented.
A fix has been deployed to the canary environment (if the breach was caused by the new version).
All gate metrics have held within thresholds for the full soak period since the fix was deployed.
On-call engineer and rollout owner have signed off on resumption.
Monitoring dashboard has been checked for any secondary anomalies.

A paused rollout should not remain paused indefinitely. If the root cause cannot be resolved within a defined window, the rollout should be aborted and the canary traffic returned to zero — not because the service is broken, but because an unresolvable pause is effectively a failed rollout.
Escalate to engineering lead; decide abort or continue
Abort rollout — return to zero, schedule post-mortem
Automatic abort if Redis pause flag not refreshed

Rollout velocity is not a metric. A rollout that pauses for 2 days because the team cannot decide whether to abort has failed operationally even if the new version is technically acceptable. Define the pause limit in the rollout plan, not during the incident.
A rollback is not the end of the deployment — it is the start of the investigation. The canary version is not discarded; it is put on hold until the root cause is understood and fixed.
Confirm rollback is complete: 0% of traffic is reaching the canary version (verify via feature flag and metrics).
File an incident ticket with the triggering metric, threshold, and observed value.
Assign a root cause investigation owner within 1 hour.
Run the eval suite against the canary version in a staging environment to reproduce the failure.
Identify whether the regression is in the model, the prompt, the parser, or the eval harness itself.
Fix the regression and run the full eval suite in staging before re-entering the rollout pipeline.
Schedule a post-mortem to review whether the trigger thresholds detected the problem early enough and whether any threshold needs tightening.

Rollout pause and rollback criteria must be defined before the rollout begins, not during an incident. For GenAI services, the critical signals are eval pass rate, safety flag rate, P99 latency, HTTP error rate, and daily token cost projection — each with specific Advisory, Major, and Critical thresholds. Critical-tier responses must be automated to achieve sub-2-minute rollback time. A paused rollout has a defined resolution window; if the root cause cannot be fixed within that window, the rollout is aborted. After any rollback, a structured post-mortem determines whether the thresholds caught the problem early enough or need to be tightened.
